coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic insight into the catalytic activities of metallic sites on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for CO2 hydrogenation

coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoudi, Armin
Movahed, Siyavash Kazemi
Farrokhpour, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: The origin of selectivity and activity of the CO2 hydrogenation reaction on single-atom catalysts composed of three adjacent 3d transition metals (Fe, Co, and Ni) supported on N-doped graphene quantum dots were systematically investigated and compared using density functional theory (DFT) calculations, natural bond orbital (NBO), and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis. This study reveals that π-backbonding between the metal and CO2* does not occur and [CO2]δ+ species drive the reaction. The CO2* reacts with H2 via the Eley-Rideal (ER) mechanism by using the synergistic effects of the N site. The higher the partial positive charge on the C atom, the lower the Ea of the reaction. Subsequently, a tautomerization reaction, which is facilitated by hydrogen bonding, occurs and hydrogen is transferred to HCOO* resulting in the formation of CHOOH*. This study shows the selective formation of formic acid from CO2 is accessible on these SACs and Fe-SAC is the best one between these three catalysts. Although CO2 is more inert than formic acid the H2 molecule reacts with the adsorbed formic acid more difficult than the adsorbed CO2. It is because the hydrogenation of formic acid causes C-H bond formation resulting in failure of the coordinated O atom's octet and the unstable H2COOH* is formed. This step is the rate-determining step of HOCH2OH formation from CO2, with Ea of 1.94, 2.03, and 2.23 eV for Fe, Co, and Ni, respectively. Finally, the system undergoes another tautomerization reaction resulting in the formation of HOCH2OH (formaldehyde monohydrate).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reaction network for CO2 hydrogenation with 2 mol of H2. First and second H2 moles were identified with red and green colors for clarity, respectively. The preferred pathway according to our calculations is shown with a bold line. In this scheme, the single and double bonds are more representative and do not express the exact bond order. LH= Langmuir–Hinshelwood, ER= Eley-Rideal mechanism.
Top and side views of the optimized structures for (a) Fe-N 4-GQD (b) Co-N 4-GQD (c) Ni-N 4-GQD.

Potential energy diagram of the optimal path for CO2* hydrogenation and the optimal structures of INs and TS on a) Fe-N 4-GQD, b) Ni-N 4-GQD, and c) Co-N 4-GQD. d) Simplified representation of conversion of CO2* to HCOO*.

Potential energy diagram of the optimal path for HCOO* hydrogenation and the optimal structures of INs and TS on a) Fe-N 3NH-GQD, b) Ni-N 3NH-GQD, and c) Co-N 3NH-GQD. d) Simplified representation of conversion of HCOO* to HCOOH*.

Potential energy diagram of the optimal path for HCOOH* hydrogenation and the optimal structures of INs and TS on a) Fe-N 4-GQD, b) Ni-N 4-GQD, and c) Co-N 4-GQD. d) Simplified representation of conversion of HCOOH* to H2COOH*.

Potential energy diagram of the optimal path for H2COOH* hydrogenation and the optimal structures of INs and TS on a) Fe-N3NH-GQD, b) Ni-N3NH-GQD, and c) Co-N3NH-GQD. d) Simplified representation of conversion of H2COOH* to HOCH2OH*.
The optimal CO2 hydrogenation approach for the HOCH2OH synthesis process on M-N 4-GQD (M= Fe, Co, and Ni). Structures of all INs and TSs are shown in SI.

Calculated bond lengths (Å) of reactants, TSs, products, and hydrogen bond donor-acceptor distance on Fe-N 4-GQD (Table 1a), Ni-N 4-GQD (Table 1b), and Co-N 4-GQD (Table 1c) in the hydrogen transfer to HCOO* step.

head.ce:title: Mechanistic insight into the catalytic activities of metallic sites on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for CO2 hydrogenation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 8415683111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 8415683111, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence authors at: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 8415683111, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The origin of selectivity and activity of the CO2 hydrogenation reaction on single-atom catalysts composed of three adjacent 3d transition metals (Fe, Co, and Ni) supported on N-doped graphene quantum dots were systematically investigated and compared using density functional theory (DFT) calculations, natural bond orbital (NBO), and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis. This study reveals that π-backbonding between the metal and CO2* does not occur and [CO2]δ+ species drive the reaction. The CO2* reacts with H2 via the Eley-Rideal (ER) mechanism by using the synergistic effects of the N site. The higher the partial positive charge on the C atom, the lower the Ea of the reaction. Subsequently, a tautomerization reaction, which is facilitated by hydrogen bonding, occurs and hydrogen is transferred to HCOO* resulting in the formation of CHOOH*. This study shows the selective formation of formic acid from CO2 is accessible on these SACs and Fe-SAC is the best one between these three catalysts. Although CO2 is more inert than formic acid the H2 molecule reacts with the adsorbed formic acid more difficult than the adsorbed CO2. It is because the hydrogenation of formic acid causes C-H bond formation resulting in failure of the coordinated O atom's octet and the unstable H2COOH* is formed. This step is the rate-determining step of HOCH2OH formation from CO2, with Ea of 1.94, 2.03, and 2.23 eV for Fe, Co, and Ni, respectively. Finally, the system undergoes another tautomerization reaction resulting in the formation of HOCH2OH (formaldehyde monohydrate).

ce:keyword.ce:text: Density functional theory: Graphene quantum dot

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays global warming is one of the most pressing geopolitical issues, and greenhouse gases (GHGs) such as CO2, play a key role in this problem [1]. Most of the world's energy is supplied by fossil fuels [2], and this causes a significant increase in the concentration of CO2 in the atmosphere, which has risen from 280 ppm before the industrial revolution to 390 ppm in 2010 [3,4]. As a result, global warming is now on the verge of exceeding the 1.5 °C limit [5], so the conversion of CO2 into chemical feedstocks achieves two goals at once [6,7]. Currently, there are environmentally friendly methods to produce hydrogen [8] and to convert CO2 into valuable products via catalytic processes [9,10]. However, these processes are not straightforward because CO2 is a nonpolar molecule with strong C = O bonds (bond dissociation energies of 178.8 Kcal/mol) resulting in its thermodynamic stability and kinetic inertness as well as, requiring the use of suitable catalysts for its activation for the process [11].

The reaction can be carried out with either heterogeneous or homogeneous catalysts [12–14]. Each of them has its advantages; homogeneous catalysis has high selectivity due to its metal atom utilization. However, most homogeneous systems suffer from poor thermal stability, difficult recovery, and toxicity due to catalyst contamination in the product. Although heterogeneous catalysts have lower selectivity than homogeneous catalysts, they do not suffer from the mentioned disadvantages of homogeneous catalysts. Increasing the metal atom utilization in a catalyst can improve the activity and selectivity of a heterogeneous catalyst [15] and this improvement can be achieved by using single atom catalysts (SACs) [16]. Due to their low-coordinated arrangement, SACs have many advantages of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts, thus providing a bridge between them [17].

The catalytic performance of most heterogeneous catalysts is closely related to the size of supported metal nanoparticles. Silaghi et al. [18] examined the CO2 activation on Ni cluster with different geometry through the density functional theory (DFT) and explored that the CO2 adsorption energy on Ni13 is −100 kJ/mol, while it is −58 kJ/mol on Ni55, therefore it is concluded that decreasing the size of Ni nanoparticles leads to stronger absorption of CO2 molecule. The concept of SACs was born with the aim of achieving the lowest size limit of supported nanoparticles. Lin et al. [19] fabricated Ir single atom supported on FeOx for water gas shift reaction (WGSR) and reported that the activity of this SAC is almost one order of magnitude higher than its cluster or nanoparticle counterparts. Also, several papers [20–22] demonstrated that dominant products on SACs are different from their nanoparticle counterparts. For example, Milet et al. [22] fabricated Ni single atom supported on MgOx catalyst and revealed that this SAC can convert CO2 molecule to CO product, and hydrogenation of CO2 to higher valuable products such as CH4 or CH3OH, is attributed to Ni clusters.

Furthermore, in SACs, support plays a critical role so that the single atom sites remain structurally stable under reaction conditions and do not aggregate. Therefore, strong bond formation between single atoms and support is essential. This strong bond causes exceptional influence on electronic and catalytic properties of SACs at atomic levels, in addition, every metal atom in SACs is in direct contact with the support while only a fraction of the metal atoms are in direct contact with the support in supported nanoparticle catalysts, thereby choosing the appropriate support for the SAC architecture is crucial. Inspired by these statements, Huang et al. [23] prepared stable monatomic dispersed Pd1/graphene and Pd1/C3N4 catalysts. They reported an enhanced hydrogenation activity of Pt in graphene support compared to C3N4 support. However, revealing the detailed mechanism for these support effects requires high-level theoretical calculations.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterials that are zero-dimensional analogs of graphene that can act as a support for nanoparticles [24]. They consist of one or a few layers of graphene sheets with lateral sizes of <20 nm and thicknesses of about 0.4–2.0 nm. The investigation of GQDs has grown considerably in recent years due to their exceptional properties such as high electron mobility and high specific surface area [25]. Also, it is possible to fabricate high transition-metal-atom loading SACs using the method based on the interconnection of GQDs. In this method proposed by Xia et al. [26], GQDs supply high surface contents for anchoring high concentrations of single atom metals and due to the steric hindrance between GQDs, agglomeration of single metal atoms that are anchored on GQDs support is inhibited and support does not undergo significant structural change during the pyrolysis process to synthesis SACs. Therefore, it can be concluded that single atoms embedded in GQD support, can act as a good model for carbon-based SACs, and the main reason for considering GQDs as support in this work is mainly due to this statement.

Wu et al. [27] prepared N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to elucidate the intrinsic activity of metal-free carbon-based thermocatalysts in CO2 hydrogenation reactions. They demonstrated CO is the predominant product at temperatures below 380 °C, and after the temperature increases to 380 °C, the predominant product turns into CH4. This CO2 activation was attributed to pyridinic type nitrogen species that can act as Lewis base site and coordinate to the C site of the CO2 molecule that acts as Lewis acid site. Therefore, anchoring single atom metal in this N-GQD support is a reasonable SAC design. Additionally, it is well known that metal-nitrogen-carbon (M-N-C) structures exhibit high stability as well as electronegative N-site that coordinate to the metal atom, significantly affecting the charge density of the metal and catalytic activity through this electronic interactions [28–30]. Furthermore, in special cases, it reported that support nitrogen can directly participate in the catalytic reaction [31].

The synthesis of well-defined monodispersed SACs is difficult, and their experimental characterization also requires advanced equipment [32,33]. Therefore, experimental mechanism evaluation of these catalysts is not affordable but the theoretical studies may shed light. For instance, based on DFT results, Hung et al. [34] tuned the electronic structure of iron SAC by using the support effect to produce the desired product. They predicted that copper-supported iron SAC can convert CO2 to methane, and similar results were obtained experimentally.

To date, however, many investigations have been done on the CO2 hydrogenation mechanism on M-Nx-C SACs, most of these studies focus on the electrochemical reduction of CO2 (eCO2RR) [27,34–37] and the main product in eCO2RR of these SACs is limited to CO. Recently, theoretical investigations [38,39] have demonstrated that N-doped graphene supported SACs may exhibit great activity and selectivity toward the hydrogenation of CO2 into other products such as formic acid (CHOOH). Nevertheless, there is a lack of systematic study that compared the trends in activity and selectivity for a narrow family of three adjacent earth-abundant metals (M=Fe, Co, Ni) M-Nx-C SACs. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, the main reason for the CO selectivity in many of these SACs has not been investigated. Furthermore, how nitrogen can directly participate in this hydrogenation reaction and what its synergistic effect is has not been evaluated. One of the newly suggested mechanisms for hydrogenation mechanism on a catalyst with a Lewis acid−base pair is synergistic activation [40]. In this type of mechanism, a charge transfer occurs from the highest occupied orbital (HOMO) of the Lewis base to the lowest unoccupied orbital (LUMO) of H2, i.e., the antibonding orbital σ*. Simultaneously, the HOMO of H2 (the bonding orbital σ) interacts with the LUMO of the Lewis acid site and consequently, the H2 molecule is dissociated. By revealing the trend of CO2 hydrogenation on these three adjacent metals in the periodic table and the support effects, it is possible to design SACs with optimal selectivity [11].

In our work, the theoretical calculation at the DFT level of theory has been performed on a metal-nitrogen-graphene quantum dot (M-N 4-GQD, M = Fe, Co, Ni) model to explore its role as SAC in the mechanism of hydrogenation of CO2. All of the intermediates (INs) and transition states (TSs) involved in the proposed mechanism have been characterized. The natural bond orbital (NBO) and quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis were also employed to elaborate the mechanism of the reaction.

All DFT calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 (G09) quantum chemistry package [41] to investigate the proposed mechanisms for the CO2 hydrogenation on the three selected SAC models (M-N-GQDs (M=Fe, Co, Ni)) from the energy point of view and compare the elementary steps of the mechanism of the three SACs. Since one of the main objectives of this study is to investigate the effect of the type of metal atom of the SAC on the CO2 hydrogenation mechanism, a reasonable description of the electronic wave function of the whole species involved in the mechanism is crucial. For this purpose, the M06–2X functional which is suitable for non-metal atoms and 3d transition metals, was selected [42]. This functional can capture the long-range interactions at the intermediate distances between two fragments (< 6 Å) which makes it a suitable density functional for mechanism evaluation and accurate estimation of the activation energies [42]. The selected basis set for the DFT calculations is def2-TZVP which is an adequate basis set for non-metals and 3d transition metals [43–45].

The structure of all reactants (M-N 4-GQD, CO2, and H2) and intermediates (INs) were optimized in the gas phase. The transition state (TSs) structures were searched by the Opt=TS keyword on the potential energy surface using the Berny optimization algorithm and the estimated TS structures were used as initial structures in the QST3 calculations to obtain accurate TS structures. The frequency calculations were performed to confirm the position of the optimized structures (reactants, products, intermediates, and TSs) on the potential energy surface (PES) in the hydrogenation pathway. Also, the intrinsic reaction coordinate (IRC) calculations were performed at the same level of theory to obtain the pre-reactants and pre-products connected to the TSs via the path with minimum energy.

To evaluate the stability of the proposed SACs, the counterpoise (CP) corrected interaction energy (Eint) between the metal atoms and N 4-GQD was calculated considering the basis set superposition error (BSSE) proposed by Boys et al. [46] (see Eq. (1)).

The QTAIM analysis was performed using the calculated wavefunction of the optimized TS and IN structures employing the Multiwfn 3.4 software [47] to determine the atomic interaction lines (AILs) between the species (H2 and CO2) and M-N-GQDs (M=Fe, Co, Ni) for better structural characterization and follow the bond breaking and forming along the reaction pathway. The wave function of the species used in the QTAIM analysis was calculated at the same level of theory that was used for the optimization of the structures. QTAIM analysis is a valuable tool for investigating the nature, strength, and properties of a diverse range of intermolecular interactions and interatomic interactions in a molecule. This analysis has been used in our work to elucidate the origin of catalytic activity in the selected SACs. The total electron density (ρ(r)), its Laplacian (∇²ρ(r)), kinetic electron energy (G(r)), and potential electron energy density (V(r)), at the bond critical point (BCP) of each AIL, which obtained from QTAIM analysis, have been used to follow the change in the strength and nature of bonds along the reaction pathway. According to the QTAIM analysis, the existence of a BCP between two fragments indicates an interaction between these two fragments. For example, the dissociation of H2 in the CO2 hydrogenation on the selected M-N 4-GQD catalysts was investigated by comparing the ρ(r) of the H-H bond in the related TS with that of isolated H2 molecule to infer the early and late TS relative to the reactants. A decrease of >50 % in ρ(r) of the breaking H-H bond is considered indicative of a late TS, while a decrease lower than 50 % is indicative of an early TS. Additionally, for INs, where the interaction between the adsorbate and SACs is assumed to be covalent, we use QTAIM parameters to elucidate the nature of the interaction. A high value of ρ(r) (greater than 0.1 a.u.), a negative sign of the Laplacian of electron density, and a small value of G(r)/|V(r)| (<0.5) indicate a shared electron interaction (covalent interaction). Conversely, values outside the aforementioned range indicate a non-covalent interaction [48]. Spectroscopic studies have demonstrated a correlation between CO2 activation and the donor-acceptor properties of the catalyst [49], indicating the necessity for tools capable of studying this phenomenon. NBO analysis using the NBO 5.0 program [50] offers a quantitative estimate for the donor-acceptor orbital interactions of different fragments of a system [51] and additionally provides partial charges of atoms as NBO charges, which have been demonstrated to be highly reliable [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural model, electronic structure and stability of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The ideal catalyst for CO2 hydrogenation should possess active sites for adsorbing and dissociating CO2 and H2 molecules. However, in most carbon-based SACs only one active site is available, resulting in the major product being CO. Consequently, it is challenging to obtain other hydrogenated products [53]. Previous literature has indicated that the presence of the pyridinic-type nitrogen sites in N-GQD plays a significant role in CO2 hydrogenation [27]. Therefore, the anchoring of metal in the cavity of N-GQD can create a system with more than one active site without diminishing metal utilization. Moreover, the formation of an NH site in the vicinity of the metal site is likely to enhance the hydrogenation of CO2 due to the potential for the formation of a hydrogen-bonded pre-complex and also nitrogen atoms may donate electrons into GQD, thereby activating adsorbent species [54].

In this work, we considered four pyridinic-type nitrogen sites in the structure of selected N-GQD (see Fig. 1 ) so that this structure can capture one metal atom (metal = Fe, Co, Ni) coordinated to four N atoms creating a square flat geometry around the metal atom. Notably, this flat geometry maximizes the metal-support electronic interaction. These arguments are further confirmed by the data from the literature, which demonstrates that the M-N 4-GQD (M=Fe, Co, Ni) optimized structures have a flat geometry with high stability. Polderon et al. [38] evaluated the structure of a single Fe and Ni atom incorporated into a nitrogen-doped graphene with three nitrogen atoms (N 3-G) and demonstrated that the structure around the metal center is not flat and that the metal atom placed outward of the graphene plane [38]. First, the N 4-GQD was optimized and then the metal atom was placed in the hole of the N 4-GQD. The SAC structure was optimized again to achieve the maximum interaction between metal atom and N atoms and obtain the most stable structures. Fig. 1 shows the optimized structures of M-N 4-GQD (M=Fe, Co, Ni) considered in our work from two different views.

Three optimized SACs, with optimized Fe-N, Co-N, and Ni-N bond lengths of 1.93, 1.92, and 1.90 Å, respectively, had a planar structure. The calculated NBO charges of their metal atoms (M=Fe, Co, Ni) are 0.85, 0.79, and 0.76e, respectively, showing the charge transfer from the metal atom to the N4-GQD, which is the maximum for the Fe atom. In a comparison of M-N 4-GQD with the M-N 3-G structure [38], a higher charge transfer from the metal center to the N 4-GQD structure is seen, which indicates a stronger interaction between the metal atom and the support. The calculated interaction energy between the metal atom and N 4-GQD for the M-N 4-GQD (M=Fe, Co, Ni) are −7.81, −6.09, and −8.04 eV, respectively. These values indicate the Ni-N 4-GQD is the most stable system among these three SACs.

Experimental studies have also demonstrated that Ni-N 4-G exhibits remarkable stability under harsh conditions and high temperatures [55]. Based on our calculations, this stability could be attributed to the strong interaction energy of Ni with the N4 site. Since the calculated interaction energy of Fe with the N4 site in Fe-N 4-GQD is comparable to that of nickel in Ni-N 4-GQD, it can be guessed that Fe-N 4-GQD is also likely to be stable in harsh conditions. Also, there is an agreement between our calculated values of the interaction energy of Fe, Ni, and Co atoms with the N4 site of N 4-GQD in the selected M-N 4-GQD catalysts and the findings of Kropp et al. [56] that Ni is the most stable single atom among the three metals in M-N4-G catalyst (M=Fe, Co, Ni). This agreement can indicate that a GQD can act as a good model for a graphene system.

A comprehensive evaluation to identify the most optimal routes for hydrogen transfer to CO2 is presented in this part. For this purpose, a thorough examination of the reaction network depicted in Scheme 1 is performed. In each step, the most stable configuration of the species on the adsorbent (M-N 4-GQD) for reactants, products, and TSs was identified. Additionally, all possible hydrogenation pathways for the final product were evaluated to ensure that the final product does not undergo further hydrogenation, however, these efforts are not reflected in the reaction network.

In this section, the various pathways of the adsorption of all related reactant species on M-N 4-GQD are presented. There are two possible pathways for the adsorption process: the first pathway involves the adsorption of H2 molecules, followed by the adsorption of CO2, while the second pathway involves the opposite order.

Fe-N4-GQD. In the first pathway, it is assumed that the activation of H2 molecule occurs via its adsorption on the Fe site of the Fe-N4-GQD catalyst (Eads= −0.52 eV). During this adsorption, a slight lengthening of the H-H bond length (+0.02 Å) for the adsorbed H2 molecule takes place compared to the bond length of the isolated H2 molecule (0.74 Å). This means that the activation of H2 molecule for its dissociation on the catalyst is not efficient.

On the other hand, occupying the metal active site of Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst by the H2 molecule stops the possibility of adsorption of CO2 on the catalyst. In this case, the only possibility for the hydrogenation of CO2 is its reaction with the activated H2 molecule without its adsorption on the catalyst. Since the increase in the bond length of adsorbed H2 molecule compared to isolated H2 is negligible, this reaction is impossible or has considerable activation energy. Of course, it is necessary to mention that some catalysts select the first pathway for the hydrogenation of CO2. For example, Sun et al. [57] reported a Rh-based catalyst for CO2 hydrogenation reaction that the first step is the dissociation of the H2 molecule on the surface via its interaction with the Rh atom so that the bond length of adsorbed H2 on the catalyst reaches 0.96 Å showing it can easily dissociate and subsequently, this activated H2 can react with the CO2 molecule.

Considering the above explanations, it seems that the adsorbing of CO2 as a primary elementary step is essential for the progression of the CO2 hydrogenation reaction on this catalyst (the second pathway).

In this pathway, following the adsorption of the CO2 molecule onto the Fe site of Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst, the H₂ molecule can be absorbed in two different ways, which together facilitate the progression of the reaction. The H2 molecule can dissociate through its adsorption on two adjacent N-sites. Then the adsorbed CO2 (CO2*) reacts with the H atoms according to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) mechanism. Alternatively, the gaseous H2 molecule can react directly with the CO2* molecule before adsorption on the catalyst (the Eley-Rideal (ER) mechanism) [39].

The calculated E ads for the adsorption of CO2 molecule on the Fe site of Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst is −0.53 eV which is equal to the E ads of the adsorption of H2 on the same site (−0.52 eV). This means H2 and CO2 do not compete for adsorption on the Fe site of the catalyst. To determine the nature of the interaction between the Fe SAC and CO2*, the QTAIM topological parameters of the BCP of the AIL between CO2 and Fe (G(r)/|V(r)|, ρ(r) and the sign of the Laplacian of the ρ(r)) were determined (1.004, 0.03628 (a.u.) and positive, respectively). These results reveal that the interaction between CO2* and the Fe site is not a covalent interaction, despite having a relatively high interaction energy.

Regarding the LH mechanism, the activation energy (Ea) required for the direct dissociation of the H2 molecule is 5.26 eV, which is a considerable amount of energy and therefore not feasible at moderate temperatures. Therefore, the ER mechanism is the preferred option, which is discussed in more detail in Section 3.2.3.

Ni-N4-GQD. In the Ni-N 4-GQD catalyst, the initial pathway (see Scheme 1) is not viable due to weaker adsorption of H2 molecule on the catalyst compared to the CO2 molecule. The calculated value of Eads for H2 and CO2 molecules is −0.058 and −0.16 eV, respectively. Consequently, only the second pathway is feasible.

Following the adsorption of the CO₂ molecule on the Ni site, our calculations show that the adsorption and dissociation of H₂ molecule by the nitrogen sites is not possible, and the reaction proceeds exclusively via the ER mechanism. There are no significant changes in the O-C-O angle, C-O bond length, and NBO partial charges of CO2 compared to those of isolated CO2. In the next step, the H2 molecule reacts directly with the CO2* in the gas phase as ER mechanism. Notably, in the NBO analysis of this system, the donor-acceptor feature between CO2 and Ni is not observed due to the electron-rich transition metal character of Ni (d 10 electronic configuration) that can't accept electrons from CO2*.

Co-N4-GQD. In the first pathway on this SAC, H2 can dissociate on the two adjacent N sites. The calculated value of E a for the dissociation of H2 molecule is 4.69 eV Since the E a value of H2 dissociation is high, this process isn't available under moderate conditions. Alternatively, as the second pathway, CO2 adsorption is the first step and is an endothermic process with the E ads of 0.69 eV The H2 molecule then reacts directly with CO2* as an ER mechanism, which will be discussed in detail in section 3.3.3. as the main hydrogenation path on Co-N 4-GQD catalyst. In this CO2 adsorption, there are no significant differences in the O-C-O angle, C-O bond length, and NBO charges of CO2 compared to the structure of isolated CO2.

The observed adsorption trend of CO2 on the metal centers of the selected catalyst can be interpreted using the d-band model [58]. Based on this model, the increase in the number of electrons of the d orbital of a metal atom is accompanied by a decrease in the adsorption energy. This model suggests that the Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst may exhibit higher E ads than the other two metal atoms (Ni and Co). However, this model is unable to account for Co-N 4-GQD catalyst since the Co system possesses a doublet multiplicity, whereas Fe and Ni are closed shells. Bhattacharjee et al. [58] presented an improved model of the d-band theory that considers the spin effect. This model indicates that the adsorption of non-magnetic species on a catalyst with majority spin electrons results in strong repulsion and a decrease in Eads. Consequently, the aforementioned models provide a reasonable explanation for the adsorption behavior of CO2 on the metal centers of the selected catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen transfer to CO2 on M-N 4-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the hydrogenation of CO2 can proceed through two mechanisms, LH and ER. The LH mechanism isn't possible in these SACs, because of the extremely high Ea value for H2 dissociation on N sites. The ER mechanism which is possible for proceeding the reaction, is investigated. A straightforward model for the mechanism of this step has been shown in Fig. 2 d.

NBO analysis of the isolated CO2 and CO2* could be used to show how the CO2 molecule is activated via its adsorption on the metal center of a catalyst for the reaction with H2. In most of the compounds where there is bonding between the metal and carbonyl group (via its O or C atom), the formation of π-type orbital interaction between vacant p-orbital of the C or O atom of carbonyl and occupied d-orbital of metal atom occurs (metal back bonding) [42]. The comparison of the NBOs of CO2* with CO2 shows a vacant non-bonding orbital with the p character on the C atom of CO2* which is absent in the isolated CO2 molecule. This orbital cannot be populated via metal back bonding according to the NBO analysis of the orbital interactions between the Lewis and non-Lewis orbitals that does not show an electron transfer from metal to CO2*. Therefore, it seems that this vacant orbital on the C atom of CO2* plays a main role in its interaction with H2 molecule. It should be mentioned that this is one of the important findings of our work which will be explained in the next section.

Fe-N4-GQD. Based on the presence of a vacant orbital on the C atom of CO2*, this atom as the Lewis acid site is the center of the dissociation reaction of the H2 molecule, and the bonding of the H atom to the C atom of CO2* occurs. In this manner, the H₂ molecule undergoes concerted dissociation on N-pyridinic and the C of CO2*, forming a six-membered cyclic transition state with an energy barrier of 1.59 eV (see Fig. 2a). This reaction results in the exothermic formation of HCOO* and NH, which releases an energy of 0.28 eV As previously mentioned, the adsorption of CO2 on the Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst is exothermic which provides the energy that can help to overcome the energy barrier of the dissociation of H2 in the ER mechanism.

To evaluate the electronic properties of the TS of H2 dissociation in Fig. 2a, the NBO and QTAIM analyses were employed. Comparison of the calculated ρ(r) of H-H bond in the TS structure (0.180 a.u.) with that of the isolated H2 molecule (0.268 a.u.), it can be concluded that the ρ(r) of the H-H bond is reduced by 32 % compared to the free hydrogen molecule. Therefore, it can be concluded that this TS represents an early TS.

The NBO analysis of the TS structure identified two significant CTs, (a) is the first CT that originated from the bonding orbital of H2 to a non-bonding orbital of the C atom in CO2* (σ H-H —>n C ) with an energy of 157.24 kcal/mol and (b) is the second CT that originated from the Lp orbitals of N-pyridinic to the anti-bonding orbital of H-H (nN —> σ* H-H ), with an energy of 71.59 kcal/mol, that this type of activation mechanism is known as synergistic activation and pyridinic-type nitrogen, and the C atom of CO2* act as Lewis base and Lewis acid, respectively [40].

Ni-N4-GQD. Concerning the ER mechanism, the hydrogenation of CO₂ is only feasible from the formate path and leads to the formation of HCOO* and NH, endothermically (ΔE = 0.97 eV) and an energy barrier of 2.09 eV (see Fig. 2b).

The observed differences between the Fe-N 4-GQD and Ni-N 4-GQD catalyst in this step of ER mechanism can be explained via the weakening of the π-bond of the C = O bond which results in a decrease in the M-O length between the metal center of the catalyst and the O atom of CO2*. As indicated in Table S1, the decrease is seen in the Fe-O (2.15 to 1.99 Å) and Ni-O (3.13 to 2.41 Å) bond length.

This observation is in a direct correlation between the ΔE of this step and the oxophilicity of metals. Since iron is more oxophilic than nickel [59], the formation of formate on iron and nickel is exothermic and endothermic, respectively. The TS of this step is similar to the TS of formate formation on the Fe-N 4-GQD, with the notable exception that the TS for Ni-N 4-GQD is a late transition state. The calculated value of ρ(r) of the H-H bond of the TS is equal to 0.128 a.u. which shows a decrease of 52.06 % compared to the ρ(r) value of isolated H2 molecule which confirms a late TS for Ni-N 4-GQD compared to Fe-N 4-GQD which shows an early TS. Therefore, according to Hammond's postulate, the Ea of this step is lower for the Fe system than for the Ni system. A comparison of the NBO analysis for this system with that of the Fe system is not particularly fruitful. Since this TS for the Ni system is a late transition state and has a different set of CTs compared to an early transition state.

Co-N4-GQD. The reaction of the free H2 molecule with the CO2* on the Co-N 4-GQD catalyst via the ER mechanism is exothermic (ΔE= −1.10 eV, see Fig. 2c). As seen, the reaction between the H₂ molecule and CO2* undergoes via a six-membered cyclic transition state with an energy barrier of 0.51 eV, forming HCOO* and NH, releasing 1.10 eV of energy due to the disruption of the symmetrical structure of CO2 which increases the interaction of adsorbate with Co site and causes the stabilization of the TS relative to the reactant. According to Table S1, the Co-O bond length decreases from 2.91 Å to 2.15 Å showing the Co-O bond becomes stronger.

In the TS structure, the calculated value of ρ(r) of the H-H bond is equal to 0.146 a.u., which represents a 45 % decrease compared to that of the isolated H-H bond. Consequently, the TS of this step is an early transition state according to Hammond's postulate and has a lower value of E a than the that of Ni system that has a late transition state.

To compare the E a of this step for Co-N 4-GQD with that of Fe-N 4-GQD, the NBO analysis was employed. Since the TSs of both systems are early transition states, the donor-acceptor characteristics of the two systems are comparable. Two significant CTs are seen in the TS of this step for Co system including, (a) the orbital interaction between the bonding orbital of H2 and the non-bonding orbital of the C atom in CO2* (σ H-H —>pC ) with the energy of 108.56 kcal/mol and (b) is the interaction between the Lp electrons of the N-pyridinic atom with the anti-bonding orbital of H2 (nN —> σ* H-H ) with the energy of 59.79 kcal/mol, therefore this CO2 activation is synergistic activation type. The corresponding orbital interaction values known as (a) and (b) for the Fe system are 157.24 and 71.59 kcal/mol, respectively. A decrease is seen in the orbital interaction energies for the Co system compared to the Fe system which leads to the lower stabilization energy for the TS of the Co system. Based on this observation, a higher E a is expected for the Co system compared to Fe system but the reverse trend is seen. Therefore, it seems that the lower E a of the Co system compared to the Fe system could be more related to more instability of the CO2-Co-N 4-GQD compared to CO2-Fe-N 4-GQD due to the lower interaction energy of the CO2 with the Co-N 4-GQD compared to that of Fe-N 4-GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen transfer to HCOO* on M-N 3Nh-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: In this step, the H atom is transferred from the NH to HCOO*, which is considered an LH mechanism. The hydrogen atom can bond to either the C or O atoms of the HCOO*. The transfer of the H atom to oxygen is expected to occur with a relatively low energy barrier and to be exothermic due to the formation of a weakly bound complex between the NH and O atom [60] and the formation of an aromatic pyridine ring in the TS structure. A straightforward model for the occurrence of this step has been shown in Fig. 3 d.

This step can be considered a tautomerization process, which allows the hydrogen atom to be transferred between two electronegative atoms (O and N) that are connected indirectly via hydrogen bonding. In the TS of this step, the N and O atoms can approach each other at a distance shorter than the sum of their respective van der Waals radii of 2.65 Å (O-H-N van der Waals radius). This reduction in distance results in the formation of a strong hydrogen bond known as a low-barrier hydrogen bond (LBHB). The energy of the formation of LBHBs is six times higher than that of ordinary hydrogen bonds in the gas phase [61].

Fe-N3NH-GQD. The calculated values of the energy barrier and ΔE of the H transfer from the NH to the uncoordinated O site of HCOO* are −0.11 and −0.46 eV, respectively (see Fig. 3a). As seen in Fig. 3a and Table 1 a, the change in the Fe-O, O-H, and N-H bond length from the reactant to TS is negligible which shows that the geometry of the TS is very similar to that of the reactants. Therefore, according to Hammond's postulate, this TS is an early one that shows an exothermic reaction. The negative value of energy barrier could be attributed to the presence of strong LBHB between the N (H-donor) and O atom (H-acceptor). The distance between the N and O atoms contributing to the LBHB bond is 2.53 Å This result is in agreement with those reported by Meligy et al. [62] that observed an energy barrier of −0.061 eV for the tautomerization of ketoenamine to the enolimine form in the gas phase. It is important to note that the calculated barrier height energy (−0.11 eV≈2.53 kcal/mol) is within the range of the RMSD error of the M06–2X method, therefore it is more acceptable to consider this step as a barrierless reaction [42].

Ni-N3NH-GQD. The H atom of the NH site is transferred to the uncoordinated oxygen atom of the HCOO* with the 0.017 eV energy barrier. This step could be considered a barrierless reaction like the corresponding step on the Fe-SAC system. The ΔE of this tautomerization is equal to −0.99 eV as shown in Fig. 3b. according to Table 1b, LBHB is seen in the TS structure which can justify the low energy barrier of this step.

Co-N3NH-GQD. Similar to Ni and Fe systems, the H transfer occurs from the NH of the HCOO-Co-N 3NH-GQD to the uncoordinated O atom of the HCOO* interacting via hydrogen bonding with it. As shown in Fig. 3c, the TS of this step is more stable than the reactant (−0.047 eV) showing a barrierless step similar to Ni and Fe systems and releasing the energy of 0.54 eV to form HOCHO*. According to Table 1c, the geometry of this TS is strikingly similar to that of the reactant. In both cases the LBHB is present, but in the TS the LBHB is more pronounced than in the reactant, resulting in a slightly more stable TS structure.

Based on the results obtained for Fe, Ni, and Co-N 4-GQD catalysts for the formation of formic acid, the first step is the rate-determining step. The Fe-N 4-GQD catalyst is the most appropriate system for formic acid formation due to the lowest value of E a with considering Eads of CO2 (Ea= 1.59 eV and Eads= −0.53 eV). Ghara and Chattaraj [63] have demonstrated that the formation of formic acid from CO2(g) and H2(g) molecules in the absence of a catalyst is subject to an energy barrier of 3.6 eV, which serves to substantiate the effectiveness of our selected catalysts for hydrogenation of CO2 to formic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen transfer to HCOOH on the M-N 4-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of the second mole of H2 molecule to HCOOH can be performed at this step. Similar to the first hydrogenation step, we solely consider the ER mechanism in this step. A straightforward model for the occurrence of this step has been shown in Fig. 4 d.

Fe-N4-GQD. In this step, the H atom can only be transferred to the carbon atom of the adsorbed formic acid, forming H2COOH* and NH (see Fig. 4a). Hydrogen transfer to oxygen atoms of formic acid is not possible because one of the O atoms is in direct interaction with the Fe atom of catalyst and the other has been saturated. As seen in Fig. 4a, this step is endothermic (ΔE= 0.56 eV), and a decrease of 46.1 % is seen in the ρ(r) of the BCP of the H2 molecule in TS (0.144 a.u.) compared to isolated H2 molecule showing an early TS for this step with the energy barrier of 1.94 eV

As shown in Figure S9, the TS of this step is a six-membered cyclic transition state similar to the TS of the first hydrogenation step (Section 3.2.3), therefore the reaction mechanism of the second mole of H2 with CHOOH*, also proceeds through synergistic activation mechanism.

In comparison to the first hydrogenation step, the instability of the product in this step is related to the failure of the octet rule for the O atom interacting with the Fe site. This failure of octet configuration of the O atom is due to the saturation of the C atom because its bonding with the H atom makes a single bond between the C and O atoms. In this case, according to Table S1, the Fe-O bond length decreases in the structure of the product (1.95 Å) compared to the reactant (2.04 Å) to compensate for the electron deficiency of the O atom that does not obey the octet rule.

The calculated topological QTAIM parameters of the Fe-O bond including G(r)/|V(r)| (0.9432), ρ(r) (0.08183 a.u.), and the sign of the Laplacian of electron density (positive) show the absence of the covalent bond between Fe and O. Hence, the coordinated O is not an octet atom and causes instability in the product compared to the reactants.

Ni-N4-GQD. The transfer of the H atom to the O or C atom of the carbonyl of HCOOH* (connected to the Ni atom) is possible via the dissociation of the H2 molecule based on the ER mechanism. In this system, the transfer of the H atom to the coordinated O site is possible since the interaction between the O site and Ni is very weak and this O could react with the H atom (in contrast to the Fe system).

The dissociation of H2 molecule via its interaction with the O atom is accompanied by the elimination of the C = O bond in the TS structure, and it causes the breaking of the octet rule for the C atom. Subsequently, this circumstance causes much instability, so that this reaction has an energy barrier of 3.88 eV and the ΔE of the reaction is equal to 3.80 eV

Alternatively, the dissociation of the H2 molecule by the interaction with the C atom of formic acid has a TS with an energy barrier of 2.27 eV, forming H2COOH* and NH (see Fig. 4b). The reaction via this approach is endothermic with the ΔE of 1.58 eV This indicates that the dissociation of H2 via the interaction with the C atom of formic acid is more feasible than interaction with the O atom. A decrease of 54.1 % was observed for the ρ(r) of H-H bond in the TS structure (0.123 a.u.) of the second route compared to isolated H2 molecule indicating a late TS structure for this reaction. Similar to the Fe system, the instability of the product compared to the reactants could be attributed to the electron deficiency of the non-octet O atom coordinated with the Ni atom.

Co-N4-GQD. Similar to Fe-N 4-GQD and Ni-N 4-GQD catalysts, the dissociation of the H2 molecule and the hydrogen transfer to formic acid via the ER mechanism on the Co-N 4-GQD catalyst, occurs via the interaction of H2 with the C atom of formic acid. The calculated barrier energy and ΔE of this step is 2.03 eV and 1.15 eV, respectively (see Fig. 4c). The ρ(r) of H-H in the geometry of TS is equal to 0.158, so it shows a 41 % decrease in bond order compared to isolated H2, so the structure of this TS is a six-membered cyclic early transition state.

Based on the results of this step obtained for the selected catalysts, a correlation between the barrier energy and the ΔE of this step with the stabilizing of the non-octet O atom is present. The calculated value of the M-O bond length is a determining factor for the comparison of the deviation of the O atom from the octet rule. Since the Fe-O bond length is smaller than the Ni-O and the Co-O bond length, the Fe-O bond is stronger than the Ni-O and the Co-O bonds showing the Fe atom can better stabilize the non-octet O atom.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen transfer to HOCH2O on the M-N 3Nh-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The transfer of the second H atom from the NH site to the HOCH2O* occurs in this step (see Figure S5). The coordinated O atom (non-octet O atom) is the best site of HOCH2O* for taking the H atom and forming of methanediol product. A straightforward model for the occurrence of this step has been shown in Fig. 5 d.

Fe-N3NH-GQD. The transfer of the H atom from the NH of the Fe-N 3NH-GQD to the non-octet O atom for the formation of HOCH2OH* has an energy barrier of 0.23 eV and ΔE of −0.99 eV (see Fig. 5a). According to Table S1, the increase of the Fe-O bond length occurs due to this hydrogen transfer from reactants (1.95 Å) to product (2.05 Å) and the O atom becomes octet again.
Ni-N3NH-GQD. The energy barrier of the H transfer from the Ni-N 3NH-GQD to HOCH2O* is 0.21 eV and it is an exothermic step (ΔE = −1.84 eV) (see Fig. 5b).

The more negative value of ΔE of this step for the Ni-N 3NH-GQD compared to Fe-N 3NH-GQD is related to the greater relaxation of the product relative to the intermediate structure (HOCH2O*) which the considerable increase in the Ni-O bond length (2.27 to 2.86 Å) confirms it.

Co-N3NH-GQD. The forming of the OHCH2OH* product on this system has an energy barrier of −0.05 eV, which releases 1.34 eV energy (see Fig. 5c). Therefore, this step is considered a barrierless reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption of HOCH2OH or hydrogen transfer to HOCH2OH on M-N 4-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Two different pathways were considered for the reaction of HOCH2OH (methanediol) on the M-N 4-GQD catalyst. The first pathway was a rearrangement mechanism so that the formaldehyde is the main product. Our theoretical investigations showed that the rearrangement of the methanediol on the selected catalyst does not occur. Notably, the rearrangement of the methanediol to formaldehyde on a Co complex mediated by a water molecule has been investigated by Yan et al. [64]. Therefore, this type of dissociation requires a third molecule, which is not present in our systems. The second pathway is using the third H2 molecule for the hydrogenation of methanediol via the hydrogen transfer to its OH or CH2 sites. This pathway was investigated theoretically via ER mechanism for three selected catalysts and no TS was observed for this pathway. Also, we investigated the possibility of H2 reacting directly with OHCH2OH* without the use of a catalyst. However, the extremely high Ea calculated for this type of reaction makes it impractical.

Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that the further hydrogenated species are likely to be kinetically inaccessible due to the high energy barrier for their formation and that the final product is methanediol on these three SACs. Therefore, the desorption of methanediol from the SACs as the only viable option is evaluated in the next section and the adsorption mode of methanediol on these three SACs is shown in Figure S6.

Fe-N4-GQD. HOCH2OH* desorbs from the surface of the Fe-N 4-GQD with a desorption energy of 1.28 eV to the gas phase. To analyze the suspected covalent bond between Fe and the oxygen atom causing high desorption energy, we examined the QTAIM parameters, G(r)/|V(r)|, ρ(r) and the sign of the Laplacian of the electron density is 1.0188, 0.05241 a.u. and positive, respectively, and it is concluded that this interaction is not a covalent bond. Therefore, covalent bond forming is not possible and the reason for this high desorption energy is related to other effects which are explained later in Co-N 4-GQD section.

Ni-N4-GQD. The Ni system is electron-rich due to its d 10 electronic configuration of Ni atom, which prevents methanediol from donating an electron to the surface. Consequently, the interaction between methanediol and the catalyst is weak, resulting in the evolution of methanediol to the gas phase with a desorption energy of 0.24 eV

Co-N4-GQD. In this system, the desorption energy of methanediol is equal to 1.10 eV, indicating that methanediol has a strong interaction with the Co system. Because of its high desorption energy, we evaluated QTAIM parameters that detect the nature of the interaction. G(r)/|V(r)|, ρ(r) and the sign of the Laplacian of the electron density are 0.9979, 0.02714 a.u., and positive, respectively. These results indicate that the interaction between Co and oxygen is not a covalent bond and the origin of this high desorption energy is something else. to make a meaningful comparison, it is necessary to examine the geometry of OHCH2OH* in these three systems. According to Table S1, the M-O distance in these systems is 2.05, 2.41, and 2.84 Å for M=Fe, Co, and Ni, respectively. The origin of these bond lengths can be attributed to two factors. First, the CT from the Lp of the oxygen atom to the metal, which is not possible in the nickel system, and for the Fe system this CT is strong that indicated in NBO analysis as Fe-O bond and for the Co system CT from Lp orbital of oxygen to Co non-bonding orbital has 10 Kcal/mol energy. This CT causes the methanediol to approach the metal and lose its symmetry, which in turn allows the methanediol to form a dipole. The second reason for the dipole interaction with the metal is the NBO charge of Fe and Co, which is 1.08 and 0.76, respectively. Consequently, the interaction of methanediol with the Fe site is stronger than with the Co site, and these arguments justify the trend of the desorption energy in these systems.

First, the CO2 molecule is adsorbed and coordinated to the metal via its oxygen atom. Following this adsorption, the H2 molecule reacts directly with CO2* via the ER mechanism, forming HCOO* and NH on the adjacent N-pyridinic atom. Subsequently, NH forms a hydrogen bond with an uncoordinated O atom of HCOO* and transfers to this O atom via a barrierless step to form HCOOH*, which is more strongly coordinated to the catalysts from its carbonyl oxygen than the primary CO2*.

In the subsequent step, the second equivalent H2 molecule can react with HCOOH* via the ER mechanism, forming H2COOH* and NH on the adjacent N-pyridinic atom. This unstable species then reacts with NH to form the OHCH2OH* product. Notably, this product cannot be further hydrogenated. For the sake of simplicity, this mechanism has been briefly written as CO2(g) → CO2* → HCOO* → HCOOH* → H2COOH* → OHCH2OH* → OHCH2OH(g).

A comparison of the reaction mechanism between these three SACs is shown in Fig. 6 and according to this figure, the rate-determining step in all three catalysts is the conversion of HCOOH to H2COOH. The energy barrier of this step is equal to 1.94, 2.03, and 2.27 eV for Fe, Co, and Ni SAC, respectively. In contrast, the first hydrogenation step is performed with the energy barrier of 1.59, 0.51, and 2.09 eV for Fe, Co, and Ni SAC, respectively.

Therefore, these types of SACs are more suitable for one-time hydrogenation of CO2 and the second hydrogenation step is more difficult. To gain insight into the underlying behavior of these SACs and reveal the reason for this specific selectivity, the NBO analysis was performed.

This analysis revealed that electron donation from the metal to the adsorbate is inhibited in these SACs. Consequently, the only viable pathway for CO2 activation is the donation of an electron to the adsorbent (SACs), forming the CO2 δ+ species. This phenomenon is more pronounced in Fe SAC due to its lower valence electrons compared to Co and Ni SACs.

The formation of this electron-deficient intermediate leads to a unique selectivity in these SACs, which serves as a valuable guide for the design of other catalysts, and partial positive charge on the C atom of adsorbate can used as a descriptor.

The ER mechanism is the predominant mechanism in these systems, as evidenced by the present NBO analysis of the TS structures. This analysis demonstrates that the N-pyridinic donates electrons to the anti-bonding orbital of the H 2 molecule, subsequently donating electrons from the bonding orbital of the H-H bond to the electron-deficient orbital on CO2*, known as synergistic activation mechanism and the M-O bond is promoted. All of these electron transfers occur in the six-membered cyclic transition state formed in ER steps.

The results demonstrated that the role of the support is not limited to the stabilization of single atoms. The support plays a pivotal role in the mechanism, as evidenced by the following observations: first, the N-pyridinic donates an electron to H2 in the ER step, causing the dissociation of the H2 molecule and allowing the binding of H to the N-pyridinic site. Second, the NH site formed can easily hydrogenate the O site of the adsorbate due to the presence of a hydrogen-bonded pre-complex.

The results of this study suggest that M-N 4-GQD SACs may have the potential to act as a thermal catalyst for the CO2 hydrogenation reaction. In addition, the study provides atomic-level insights into the critical role of the N-doped support, the electronic behavior of the SACs, and the reason behind the selective hydrogenation of CO2 on these catalysts. These findings can be used in the design of highly efficient thermal catalysts.
